summary: Jeff Atwood addresses a common frustration at tech conferences: presenters showing code in tiny, unreadable fonts. Writing from VSLive! 2006 San Francisco, he calls out speakers who force audiences to squint at 10-12 point IDE text. He recommends using magnification utilities, including Windows XP's built-in magnifier, and provides practical tips for using it effectively during presentations. The post is a brief but pointed reminder that presenter convenience shouldn't come at the expense of audience comprehension.
key_points: Presenters frequently make the mistake of showing code in fonts too small for audiences to read
Windows XP includes a built-in magnifier utility (magnify.exe) that's sufficient for most presentation needs
The magnifier can dock to screen edges, follow mouse/keyboard actions automatically, and scale from 1x to 9x
Pre-scaling fonts or using magnification tools should be standard practice for any code demonstration
money_quotes: I've spent a disproportionate amount of that time staring at tiny, unreadable 12 and 10 point IDE text.
Presenters, please don't do this to your audiences.
Everything I do is now perfectly visible – even from the last row of the room.
key_insight: Technical presenters have a responsibility to ensure their code is readable by using magnification tools, because audience comprehension matters more than presenter convenience.
